item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find myself agreeing with a lot of the DV's on here. There's not a lot of substance to this other than "cartoon tropes happening in real life" and there doesn't really seem to be much else.

As someone who is writing a "cartoon trope happening in real life" SCP right now, I think this misses one of the most important steps when writing one of these, and that's twisting and realizing the ideas shown here.

I feel it would have been a lot more interesting if this article where to have gone into detail as to how this would affect Researcher Rex in the long run. I can imagine that having to go through 100 tests of bizarre and insane cartoon logic would definitely take somewhat of a mental toll on a person.

This is charming, but I will sadly have to -1 for these reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the same as skips that simply grab a pop-culture item/character and paste it into an article. There is nothing beyond it pointing towards a specific trope and saying "hey guys, remember this wacky stuff?". Hell, Poky Pig straight up appears at the end. It doesn't even have the elements that make this type of slapstick funny. No sound effects, exaggerated animations, or characters being irritated and dumbfounded at the extreme cartoon logic. I actually thought at first this was going to go the route of a horror-esque article where the cartoon physics are taken to their logical extreme and being lethal in-universe.

-1. I'm having a hard time finding any redeeming qualities in this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a great idea and while I understand some saying it was too short I think the length was good as the nature of the SCP was explored. If it had gone longer it might have outplayed itself.

Great job +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find myself agreeing with Shaggy. It just felt very predictable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the ending was kind of a send off (albeit 17 years late). It mourns the passing of someone who made a lot of our childhoods brighter and I love it for that reason.

I would argue that it does absolutely nothing of the sort. It's an ending, sure, but it's merely an ending. Plain and simple. There isn't a shred of pathos, meaning, or impactfulness that can be gleaned from it even with a generous reading.

Perhaps, had there been some form of postscript that held any amount of thoughtfulness or reflection, or anything to give at least some emotive weight, I would be inclined to see that as a valid observation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree (although this may be loony toons nostalgia goggles). I think the ending was kind of a send off (albeit 17 years late). It mourns the passing of someone who made a lot of our childhoods brighter and I love it for that reason.

You have a damn good point though. Describing physical humor often does just suck the fun right out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this. I'm not sure what differentiates this from the "pop culture thing but an SCP" that get badly coldposted and the guides tell people not to do.

It's especially bad because the tropes it relies on are ones where the charm is largely derived from the visuals and sounds. I'm not an entirely humourless killjoy - classic Warner Bros cartoons are hilarious - but describing them in text is the best way to suck all the life out of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm genuinely disappointed to have checked out top rated for content to discover its (current) top slot is occupied by what is perhaps one of the most unoriginal pieces of (positively rated) content I've come across on this site in the last several years — being in essence a list of tropes written in pseudo-clinical style with the absolute bare minumum of window-dressing to qualify it as an article.

Apart from the faintest tinge of nostalgia, I gained nothing from this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t know but something about seeing the head of a photorealistic porky pig pop out from between the curtains gets me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this was an absolute pleasure to read

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is unapologetically goofy in a good way. I have some problems- I reiterate the problem of an anti-climax earlier in the thread- but aside from those this earns a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well written. A little short, but good nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed reading this, but it seems to fit in better as a -J than a mainlist SCP. Still good enough to upvote, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair! And I also used the word "rebuke". Probably should have thrown in the word "feckless" to make it extra obvious I was joking.

Humor is hard to objectively measure. I have a friend who will literally double over with laughter at the word "poot", so who's to say. I found the repetition and absurdity in this article to be very funny, evoking the mental image of these scientists constantly rushing the door in Benny Hill-esque attempt to breach the anomaly. Not for everyone, but I laughed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with the the statement that humour can manifest in different ways and mainlist anomalies can be funny, although the sentence about peons feels pretty presumptuous, even as a joke.

I think my personal problem with this piece is that it's simply not funny, being too blunt to be a parody/reference and too simple to be humorous on its own.

item.title: Site Crit Team
item.content:encoded:
This is definitely a mainlister and not a -J, and this is a good example of something that gets in, makes its point, and gets out.

While I found this to be enjoyable, I want to reiterate that the strength of this article is in the precision of the words rather than the substance of the story. It's got a narrative that is a fitting tribute to the subject material, and we don't cheapen and part of it with an overly flowery point of view, or unnecessary cynicism, or clinical sterilization. Its humor is in-line with Saturday morning cartoons and, while the logs do start to pile up, they definitely stay short of carrying on for too long.

Great piece, Nico. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of course this was a Nico skip

Way to make me smile ❤️

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rebuke all these peons claiming this should have been a -J. A -J is a joke disguised as an article, this is just an article that happens to be very funny. Articles, and by extension anomalies, are allowed to be funny! It's written clinically, it's got good real-world correlations, and as someone who works in animation it makes me very happy.

Big +1. Nice work uncle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is the very definition of a - J, but it is an upvote from me anyway-I couldn't stop laughing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this would be better as a -J, but have an upvote anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw how short this was and it initially threw me off then I read it and I was like 'this is amazing I love this.' I do still wish it was longer though, maybe a little more story, but other than that I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love shorter articles and this is a great example, had a good laugh, it was a great length and an overall good atmosphere to it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is too short for me, as it was previously said it's essentially a listing of common cartoon tribes. Though, I'll upvote it anyway because it got a good chuckle out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a fun little anomaly that gets in, does it's thing, and gets out.

In this case, that thing was making me remember watching cartoons as a little kid on Saturday morning. This is a great little love letter to Chuck Jones and I enjoyed reading it very much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idk how I can see the amount of time this article was revised but this was great from beginning to end it got every little troupe of a loony toons all in one great job

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too on the nose but not detailed enough to be funny, and without the suspension of disbelief afforded by a truly funny article I have to question why the Foundation repeats this experiment one hundred times.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same, except not enough to upvote. This is a fairly bland and unoriginal anomaly that plays out in a predictable fashion, without any colour to make it feel worthwhile. It's essentially a list of cartoon tropes played straight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this up until the end, which feels too anticlimactic. Still an upvote, but a bit disappointed by that last log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
many thanks to the folks who crit this, including akimoto, mcb, aismallard, gee, kirby, nameless, and others.
special thanks to mallard for being meticulous with his feedback and to nameless for ironically naming the piece.

th-th-th-th-thats all folks!
